sections.about: This metric measures the share of investments made in related parties as a percentage of total investments. It is a Section A 'Openness of Business' / related-party governance disclosure, linked to Principle 1 governance and conflict-of-interest controls rather than a Principle 7 Essential or Leadership indicator. A lower value indicates better performance because it suggests a smaller proportion of investment activity is concentrated within the related-party ecosystem.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: In FY2022-23, Jindal Stainless led with the lowest share at 74%, followed by SAIL at 85.85%, Tata at 90%, and JSW at 99.9%. In FY2023-24, SAIL moved to the top position at 81.66%, followed by Jindal at 89%, Tata at 96%, and JSW at 99.95%. In FY2024-25, SAIL continued to lead and improved further to 78.82%, followed by Jindal at 93.04%, Tata at 97.58%, and JSW at 100%. The spread largely reflects group structure: companies with large subsidiary, joint venture, captive power, mining, downstream, or service ecosystems naturally show higher investment concentration in related entities. Tata Steel's FY2025 BRSR confirms that multiple Indian subsidiaries were merged into the standalone reporting boundary, while the company continues to disclose both standalone and consolidated ESG/governance information, which affects peer comparability. Tata also has a policy on dealing with related-party transactions mapped under its governance policies, while SAIL discloses a related-party transaction policy intended to ensure transparency, fairness, approval discipline, and conflict-of-interest management.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranks 3rd at 97.58%, behind SAIL at 78.82% (rank 1) and Jindal at 93.04% (rank 2). Tata's FY2022-23 value of 90% placed it 3rd, worsening to 96% in FY2023-24 and 97.58% in FY2024-25. Tata's position is not a direct indication of weak governance; it reflects the investment architecture of a large integrated steel group with merged and retained group entities, downstream companies, utilities, power, services, and international exposure. The main management implication is to maintain clear audit-committee oversight and arm's-length documentation rather than treat the ratio alone as an adverse governance signal.
sections.comparabilityNotes: FY2022-23 values for all four companies are prior-year comparatives reported in their respective FY2023-24 BRSRs. This metric is highly sensitive to group structure, merger history, captive-entity architecture, and whether investments are housed in operating subsidiaries, associates, JVs, or external instruments.
